CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-26-2015 Continued To Jul-08-15 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Defendant The State Bar Of Californias Special Motion To Strike Complaint Under Californias Anti-Slapp Statute [C.C.P. 425.16]
Off Calendar Feb-05-2015 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Assistant General Counsel DANIELLE A. LEE of the Office of General Counsel, Tel No. (415) 538-2517 appearing for Defendant State Bar of California;
No other appearances made in this matter.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA'S Special Motion To Strike Complaint Under Californias Anti-Slapp Statute [C.C.P. 425.16] is granted. The Court must engage in a two-step process when ruling on an anti-SLAPP motion under CCP sec. 425.16. "First, the court decides whether the defendant has made a threshold showing that the cause of action is one arising from protected activity'If the court finds such a showing has been made'it then determines whether the plaintiff has demonstrated a probability of prevailing on the claim." (Equilon Enterprises v. Consumer Cause Inc. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 53, 67.) In determining whether the allegations arise from a protected activity, the court's "focus is on the principal thrust or gravamen of the causes of action, i.e., the allegedly wrongful and injury producing conduct that provides the foundation for the claims." (Castleman v. Sagaser (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 481, 490-91.) Here, each of Plaintiff's causes of action arises out of the State Bar disciplinary proceedings brought against him or the State Bar conducting an arbitration and enforcing an arbitration award, as required by Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 6200 et seq. Thus, the first prong of the anti-SLAPP statute is met. Plaintiff has not demonstrated a probability of prevailing. A plaintiff is required to "show both that the claim is legally sufficient and there is admissible evidence that, if credited, would be sufficient to sustain a favorable judgment." (McGarry v. University of San Diego 154 Cal.App.4th 97, 108.) The only evidence submitted by Plaintiff is his declaration. The declaration restates the allegations in the complaint and cites to jury instructions. The declaration does not state facts, as required by CCP sec. 425.16(b)(2). ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Dawn Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)

To Lift A Stay On Discovery And Postpone Anti-Slapp Motion Hearing (Ccp Sec 425.16(G)
Off Calendar Jan-12-2015 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFF GREGORY CHANDLER'S Motion to Lift a Stay on Discovery and Postpone Anti-Slapp Motion Hearing (CCP Sec. 425.16(G)) is denied. CCP 425.16(g) stays discovery upon the filing of an Anti-SLAPP motion unless the Court finds "good cause" to lift the discovery stay. To satisfy the "good cause" requirement, the plaintiff must establish the viability of its claim by a prima facie showing. (Paterno v. Sup. Ct. (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 1342, 1349.) Plaintiff has not established good cause for lifting the discovery stay. He does not clearly identify what discovery is needed, nor does he explain how the evidence will establish a probability of prevailing on his causes of action. Additionally, the motion is also denied on procedural grounds. Pursuant to the Order Shortening Time issued on December 30, 2014, Plaintiff was required to serve Defendant with the motion on December 31, 2014. Plaintiff mailed the motion (which did not arrive to Defendant until January 2, 2015 due to the New Years holiday) and therefore was not in compliance with the Court's Order. PREVAILING PARTY SHALL PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)
